content.text: Flat Files & Object Storage in Data Pipelines

Data pipelines almost always begin with one fundamental question: where is the data, and what shape is it in? The most common answers involve flat files — CSV, JSON, Parquet, and similar formats — stored either on local disk or, increasingly, in cloud object storage services like AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage, or Azure Blob Storage. Mastering how to read, write, and stream these formats efficiently is one of the most practical skills in Python data engineering.
are files where data is stored as plain text or binary records in a tabular or semi-structured format, without the relational logic of a database. Each row (or JSON object) is a self-contained unit.
— tab-separated; used when data contains commas
— JavaScript Object Notation; handles nested/hierarchical data; JSONL (newline-delimited) is pipeline-friendly
— columnar binary format from Apache; compressed, schema-enforced, fast
— row-based binary; schema-embedded, great for Kafka pipelines
— columnar binary; widely used in Hive/Hadoop ecosystems
is a cloud service that stores files (called "objects") in flat namespaces inside containers called "buckets." Unlike a filesystem, there are no real folders — only key names that may contain

Flat files are the lingua franca of data exchange. APIs export JSON, databases dump CSVs, IoT sensors write logs, ML training pipelines expect Parquet. Understanding these formats is unavoidable in any real-world data pipeline.
scale and durability without managing servers

. A company might store petabytes of event logs in S3 at very low cost, then have dozens of Python workers reading slices of those files in parallel. Without understanding object storage, a pipeline is limited to local filesystems, which don't scale and aren't collaborative.
Together, flat files + object storage form the foundation of the
architecture — the dominant pattern for storing raw and intermediate data before it reaches a warehouse.
Zero infrastructure — no database server needed
Human-readable formats (CSV, JSON) are easy to inspect and debug
Language-agnostic — any tool can read them
Parquet/ORC give excellent compression and read performance for analytical workloads
Easy versioning — a file is a file; it can be stored in Git LFS, S3 versioning, or just renamed
Virtually unlimited scale — no capacity planning
High durability — S3 is designed for 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability
Cheap at rest, pay-per-request for access
Native integration with every major cloud service (Lambda, Glue, Dataproc, etc.)
Concurrent access by many readers/writers without locking
No indexing — reading one row from a 10 GB CSV requires scanning the entire file
CSV has no schema enforcement — a pipeline breaking silently because a column shifted is a classic bug
JSON nesting must be flattened manually for tabular processing
Large uncompressed text files waste bandwidth and storage
Network latency — reading from S3 is slower than reading from local NVMe disk
No partial updates — to change one row in a Parquet file, you must rewrite the entire file
Eventual consistency in some operations (though S3 is now strongly consistent for new writes)
Authentication and IAM configuration can be complex and a source of bugs
Costs can surprise — egress fees, per-request charges, and data transfer costs add up at scale
The simplest tool is the built-in

import csv

with open("sales.csv", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row["product"], row["amount"])
maps each row to a dictionary using the header row as keys. It handles quoting, escaping, and different delimiters automatically.
For larger files, use generators to avoid loading everything into memory:
Standard JSON (entire file is one object or array):

import json

with open("config.json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)  # loads entire file into memory
JSONL (one JSON object per line — pipeline-friendly):
JSONL is ideal for logs and streaming outputs because you can append a record without rewriting the file and process it line-by-line without loading it all.
Reading Parquet with PyArrow and Pandas
Parquet requires a library. The two main ones are

import pyarrow.parquet as pq
import pandas as pd

# Read entire file
df = pd.read_parquet("sales.parquet", engine="pyarrow")
print(df.head())

# Read only specific columns (columnar advantage — skips others on disk)
df = pd.read_parquet("sales.parquet", columns=["product", "amount"])

# Read in chunks using PyArrow directly
parquet_file = pq.ParquetFile("sales.parquet")
for batch in parquet_file.iter_batches(batch_size=10_000):
    df_chunk = batch.to_pandas()
    process(df_chunk)
The columnar advantage is real: if a Parquet file has 100 columns but you only need 3, only those 3 columns are read from disk. This can reduce I/O by 97%.

import csv
import json
import pandas as pd

# Write CSV
rows = [{"product": "A", "amount": 100}, {"product": "B", "amount": 200}]
with open("output.csv", "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=["product", "amount"])
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerows(rows)

# Write JSONL
with open("output.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for row in rows:
        f.write(json.dumps(row) + "\n")

# Write Parquet
df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
df.to_parquet("output.parquet", engine="pyarrow", index=False, compression="snappy")
is the official AWS SDK for Python. It is the primary way to interact with S3.
Authentication can be handled several ways — environment variables are the most portable:

export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your_key
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your_secret
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1
Or use IAM roles when running on EC2/Lambda — no credentials needed in code.

import boto3

s3 = boto3.client("s3")

# List objects in a bucket/prefix
response = s3.list_objects_v2(Bucket="my-bucket", Prefix="data/2024/")
for obj in response.get("Contents", []):
    print(obj["Key"], obj["Size"])

# Download a file to disk
s3.download_file("my-bucket", "data/2024/sales.csv", "/tmp/sales.csv")

# Upload a file from disk
s3.upload_file("/tmp/output.parquet", "my-bucket", "processed/output.parquet")

# Read file directly into memory (no temp file)
response = s3.get_object(Bucket="my-bucket", Key="data/sales.csv")
content = response["Body"].read().decode("utf-8")
Reading S3 Files Directly Without Downloading
Instead of downloading to disk first, you can stream from S3 directly into pandas or csv:

import boto3
import pandas as pd
import io

s3 = boto3.client("s3")

# Read CSV directly from S3 into a DataFrame
response = s3.get_object(Bucket="my-bucket", Key="data/sales.csv")
df = pd.read_csv(io.BytesIO(response["Body"].read()))

# Read Parquet directly from S3
response = s3.get_object(Bucket="my-bucket", Key="data/sales.parquet")
df = pd.read_parquet(io.BytesIO(response["Body"].read()))
For large files, stream in chunks instead of reading all at once:

import csv
import io

response = s3.get_object(Bucket="my-bucket", Key="data/large.csv")
stream = response["Body"]

# Stream the body as a text file
text_stream = io.TextIOWrapper(stream, encoding="utf-8")
reader = csv.DictReader(text_stream)
for row in reader:
    process(row)
Using s3fs for a Filesystem-Like Interface
to make S3 behave like a local filesystem, which integrates smoothly with pandas, PyArrow, and other libraries:

import s3fs
import pandas as pd

fs = s3fs.S3FileSystem()

# List files
files = fs.ls("my-bucket/data/2024/")

# Open file like a local file
with fs.open("my-bucket/data/sales.csv") as f:
    df = pd.read_csv(f)

# Pandas can accept s3:// URIs directly when s3fs is installed
df = pd.read_parquet("s3://my-bucket/data/sales.parquet")
df.to_parquet("s3://my-bucket/processed/output.parquet")
This URI-based syntax is one of the cleanest approaches for data pipelines because the code looks the same whether reading from local disk or S3 — just change the path.
This three-zone pattern (raw → processed → warehouse) is the backbone of modern data lakes. Python handles the middle transformation step.

When files grow large, they are partitioned — split into multiple files organized by a key like date or region. This dramatically speeds up downstream reads because queries only read the relevant partitions.

import pyarrow.dataset as ds

# Read entire partitioned dataset
dataset = ds.dataset(
    "s3://my-bucket/processed/orders/",
    format="parquet",
    filesystem=s3fs.S3FileSystem(),
    partitioning="hive"  # recognizes key=value directory names
)

# Push-down filter — only reads day=24 partitions
table = dataset.to_table(
    filter=ds.field("day") == 24,
    columns=["order_id", "amount"]
)
df = table.to_pandas()
directory is never read, saving significant I/O at scale.
Handling Large Files: Chunking and Streaming
For files too large to fit in memory, read in chunks:

import pandas as pd

chunk_iter = pd.read_csv("huge_file.csv", chunksize=50_000)
results = []

for chunk in chunk_iter:
    # process each chunk independently
    filtered = chunk[chunk["amount"] > 100]
    results.append(filtered)

final_df = pd.concat(results, ignore_index=True)

import pyarrow.parquet as pq

pf = pq.ParquetFile("huge_file.parquet")

for batch in pf.iter_batches(batch_size=100_000):
    df = batch.to_pandas()
    # process df
Streaming from S3 with Chunked Reads

import boto3
import pandas as pd
import io

s3 = boto3.client("s3")
response = s3.get_object(Bucket="my-bucket", Key="huge.csv")
body = response["Body"]

# Read in 5 MB chunks from the HTTP stream
chunk_size = 5 * 1024 * 1024  # 5 MB
buffer = b""
rows = []

for chunk in body.iter_chunks(chunk_size=chunk_size):
    buffer += chunk
    # process complete lines only
    lines = buffer.split(b"\n")
    buffer = lines[-1]  # keep incomplete last line
    for line in lines[:-1]:
        rows.append(line.decode("utf-8"))
For most use cases, the s3fs + pandas approach is simpler and good enough. Manual chunk streaming is needed for truly massive files or memory-constrained environments.
Using fsspec for Unified Filesystem Access
is a Python library that provides a uniform interface to local filesystems, S3, GCS, Azure Blob, HTTP, SFTP, and more. It's the abstraction layer used internally by pandas, Dask, and PyArrow.
pip install fsspec s3fs gcsfs adlfs

import fsspec

# Works for local, s3://, gcs://, abfs:// just by changing the path
with fsspec.open("s3://my-bucket/data/sales.csv", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()

with fsspec.open("gcs://my-gcs-bucket/data/sales.csv", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()

with fsspec.open("/local/data/sales.csv", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
This is extremely useful for writing pipelines that work both locally (during development) and in cloud environments (production) without changing code.

An e-commerce platform dumps daily order exports as CSV files to S3. A Python pipeline runs nightly, reads the CSV, validates and transforms it, then writes Parquet to a processed zone. An analytics team queries the Parquet data from a warehouse.

import boto3
import pandas as pd
import io
import logging
from datetime import date

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

RAW_BUCKET = "ecomm-raw"
PROCESSED_BUCKET = "ecomm-processed"
TODAY = date.today().isoformat()  # e.g., "2024-06-24"

def read_raw_orders(s3_client, date_str: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    key = f"orders/date={date_str}/orders.csv"
    logger.info(f"Reading s3://{RAW_BUCKET}/{key}")
    response = s3_client.get_object(Bucket=RAW_BUCKET, Key=key)
    df = pd.read_csv(io.BytesIO(response["Body"].read()))
    logger.info(f"Read {len(df):,} rows")
    return df

def validate(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    before = len(df)
    df = df.dropna(subset=["order_id", "amount", "customer_id"])
    df = df[df["amount"] > 0]
    after = len(df)
    logger.info(f"Validation: dropped {before - after} invalid rows")
    return df

def transform(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df["amount"] = pd.to_numeric(df["amount"], errors="coerce")
    df["order_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["order_date"])
    df["year"] = df["order_date"].dt.year
    df["month"] = df["order_date"].dt.month
    df["day"] = df["order_date"].dt.day
    return df

def write_processed(df: pd.DataFrame, s3_client, date_str: str):
    key = f"orders/date={date_str}/part-0001.parquet"
    buffer = io.BytesIO()
    df.to_parquet(buffer, engine="pyarrow", index=False, compression="snappy")
    buffer.seek(0)
    s3_client.put_object(
        Bucket=PROCESSED_BUCKET,
        Key=key,
        Body=buffer.getvalue()
    )
    logger.info(f"Written s3://{PROCESSED_BUCKET}/{key} ({buffer.tell():,} bytes)")

def run_pipeline(date_str: str):
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    df = read_raw_orders(s3, date_str)
    df = validate(df)
    df = transform(df)
    write_processed(df, s3, date_str)
    logger.info("Pipeline complete")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_pipeline(TODAY)

This pipeline reads CSV → validates → transforms → writes Parquet with partitioned key naming. It is idempotent (running it twice produces the same result), which is a critical property for reliable pipelines.

# BAD — loads 10 GB into RAM
df = pd.read_csv("huge_10gb_file.csv")

# GOOD — process in chunks
for chunk in pd.read_csv("huge_10gb_file.csv", chunksize=50_000):
    process(chunk)
2. Not specifying dtypes when reading CSVs
Without explicit types, pandas guesses — and guesses wrong. A column of IDs like

# BAD
df = pd.read_csv("orders.csv")

# GOOD
df = pd.read_csv("orders.csv", dtype={"order_id": str, "amount": float})
CSV files from different systems may be UTF-8, Latin-1, or Windows-1252. Not specifying encoding causes

df = pd.read_csv("file.csv", encoding="utf-8")
# If unknown, try: encoding="latin-1" or use chardet to detect

# BAD — JSONL is NOT valid JSON as a whole document
with open("events.jsonl") as f:
    data = json.load(f)  # raises JSONDecodeError

# GOOD
with open("events.jsonl") as f:
    for line in f:
        record = json.loads(line)
5. Downloading files to disk before reading
step when the data could be streamed directly into pandas. Disk writes waste time and space, especially in container environments.
6. Forgetting to paginate S3 list operations
returns a maximum of 1000 objects. If a prefix has more, the rest are silently dropped without pagination.

# BAD
response = s3.list_objects_v2(Bucket="my-bucket", Prefix="data/")
files = [obj["Key"] for obj in response.get("Contents", [])]

# GOOD — paginate
paginator = s3.get_paginator("list_objects_v2")
files = []
for page in paginator.paginate(Bucket="my-bucket", Prefix="data/"):
    for obj in page.get("Contents", []):
        files.append(obj["Key"])

# BAD — adds an unwanted integer index column
df.to_csv("output.csv")

# GOOD
df.to_csv("output.csv", index=False)

CSV is fine for input from external sources, but within a pipeline, always store intermediate results as Parquet. It is 3–10x smaller, much faster to read, and carries a schema that prevents type corruption.
Partition by date or common filter key.
directories. This enables partition pruning in downstream reads, which can reduce query time from minutes to seconds.
Any file operation that might grow over time should use generators or chunked reads from day one. Retrofitting streaming into a pipeline that assumed all data fits in RAM is painful.
to validate column presence, types, and value ranges as early as possible. Catching a malformed file at ingestion is far cheaper than finding corrupt data in a downstream report.

import pandera as pa

schema = pa.DataFrameSchema({
    "order_id": pa.Column(str, nullable=False),
    "amount": pa.Column(float, pa.Check.greater_than(0)),
    "customer_id": pa.Column(str, nullable=False),
})

validated_df = schema.validate(df)  # raises SchemaError if invalid
(better ratio). For CSV, gzip is standard — most tools read
Running a pipeline twice should produce the same output, not duplicate data. Use dated output paths and overwrite-on-write rather than append-on-write.
Use IAM roles, not hardcoded credentials.
in code or version control. Use IAM instance roles (EC2/ECS), AWS Secrets Manager, or environment variables injected at runtime.
Parallelism is the key scaling lever for object storage.
Because S3 has essentially unlimited bandwidth when accessed from many concurrent connections, reading 100 files in parallel is almost exactly 100× faster than reading them sequentially. Use

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
import boto3
import pandas as pd
import io

s3 = boto3.client("s3")

def read_parquet_from_s3(key: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    response = s3.get_object(Bucket="my-bucket", Key=key)
    return pd.read_parquet(io.BytesIO(response["Body"].read()))

keys = [f"data/part-{i:04d}.parquet" for i in range(50)]

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=16) as executor:
    dfs = list(executor.map(read_parquet_from_s3, keys))

final_df = pd.concat(dfs, ignore_index=True)

were historically 3,500 PUT and 5,500 GET requests per second per prefix. This limit no longer applies to most workloads since AWS removed it for individual prefixes in 2018, but for extreme throughput, spreading keys across multiple prefixes still helps.
Use Dask or Ray for truly large datasets.
When data doesn't fit in memory even with chunking, Dask provides a pandas-compatible API that operates lazily on distributed partitions:

import dask.dataframe as dd

# Reads all matching files lazily — nothing loaded yet
df = dd.read_parquet("s3://my-bucket/processed/orders/**.parquet")

# Filter and aggregate — Dask executes in parallel across partitions
result = df[df["amount"] > 100].groupby("customer_id")["amount"].sum().compute()
De facto standard for tabular data manipulation
Parquet and Arrow format I/O; also used by pandas internally
Alternative Parquet library; slightly faster writes in some cases
AWS SDK — S3, and every other AWS service
Unified filesystem abstraction over all of the above
Parallel, distributed DataFrames; scales pandas workflows
High-performance DataFrame library in Rust; much faster than pandas for large files
Streaming reads/writes to S3, GCS, Azure, HTTP, HDFS
Delta Lake format (versioned Parquet) for ACID transactions on object storage
is particularly handy for treating any URI as a file-like object:

from smart_open import open as smart_open

# Works for local, s3://, gs://, https:// automatically
with smart_open("s3://my-bucket/data/sales.jsonl", "r") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(json.loads(line))
No schema, slow for large data, no column pruning
Binary — not human-readable; partial updates impossible
Requires running server, harder to scale horizontally
Cost, requires loading step, overkill for small data
Schema flexibility can become schema chaos; not columnar
Large-scale data lakes needing ACID transactions
Added complexity; requires a compatible query engine

For a Python data pipeline at startup or medium scale, Parquet on S3 hits the sweet spot: low cost, no infrastructure, excellent library support, and good query performance when partitioned correctly.
Never embed credentials in code. Use IAM roles on cloud compute, environment variables or secrets managers elsewhere. Rotate access keys regularly.
By default, S3 buckets should be private. Use bucket policies to grant least-privilege access. Block public access at the account level unless explicitly needed.
S3 supports server-side encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C). Enable SSE-S3 as a minimum for all buckets. For regulated data, use SSE-KMS to control key access independently.
Enable S3 versioning on processed data buckets. This protects against accidental overwrites or deletes, and allows rollback to a previous pipeline run.
Network errors happen. Always wrap S3 calls in retry logic.
S3 returns an ETag (usually an MD5 hash) for every object. For critical data, verify the checksum after download to detect corruption:

import hashlib

response = s3.get_object(Bucket="my-bucket", Key="critical.parquet")
content = response["Body"].read()
etag = response["ETag"].strip('"')
local_md5 = hashlib.md5(content).hexdigest()
assert local_md5 == etag, "Checksum mismatch — file may be corrupt"
error under high request rates. Exponential backoff with jitter is the correct response —
's adaptive retry mode handles this automatically.
Flat files (CSV, JSON, Parquet) and object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) form the foundation of the data lake architecture and are the most common data ingestion sources in Python pipelines.
Parquet should be the default format for intermediate and processed data — it is smaller, faster, schema-enforced, and supports column pruning.
Always use streaming or chunked reads for files that may grow large; never assume data fits in memory.
is the core library for S3 access;
add a filesystem-like interface that integrates transparently with pandas and PyArrow.
S3 scales horizontally — parallel reads with
are the primary way to achieve high throughput.
Partition datasets by date or filter key; use PyArrow's dataset API for partition-aware reads that skip irrelevant files entirely.
Validate schema at the point of ingestion using
or similar; catching type errors early prevents silent data corruption downstream.
Security fundamentals — no hardcoded credentials, private buckets by default, SSE encryption, and versioning enabled — should be non-negotiable from day one.
